data.title: LGLV: A Defensive Alternative Amid Increased Market Volatility
data.description:
The SPDR SSGA US Large Cap Low Volatility Index ETF (LGLV), launched on 02/20/2013 by State Street Global Advisors and managed by SSGA Funds Management, aims to replicate the SSGA US Large Cap Low Volatility Index that consists of large-cap stocks issued in the U.S. markets that have exhibited low volatility in the past. It charges an expense ratio of 0.12% and manages about $1 billion.

With trade policy uncertainty and a Fed appearing stuck in inaction as a result, the currently increased levels of volatility are likely to remain so until it's clearer that we're moving towards a resolution. Being defensive is reasonable and to that end, LGLV could serve well investors who still want to participate in the market.

Portfolio

The number of holdings is currently 168 and idiosyncratic risk is very low as the portfolio is weighted based on volatility (lower = greater weight), instead of market capitalization.

Consistent with the low volatility bias, financial institutions have the greatest weight in the fund; their shares often exhibit consistent earnings and low beta. The Industrials sector also has a meaningful weight. Though tech stocks also have a substantial weight, the screen most likely emphasizes firms with strong balance sheets and less cyclicality for them to be considered "low-volatility".

Performance

The price investors pay beyond the fees is what some may consider a "meaningful" return differential when you compare the LGLV's performance against the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

But just to be clear, in the last decade, LGLV hasn't exactly proved to be a good alternative to a vanilla large-cap ETF like SPY on any basis. Its annualized return has been about one percentage point lower, while its standard deviation and maximum drawdown haven't been meaningfully lower. Its Sharpe has been the same as SPY's.

However, one could argue it can be useful during periods of extreme market conditions. Its worst year, which was in 2022, involved an 8.17% decrease. SPY suffered an 18.17% fall in the same year.

It comes down to whether investors are willing to underperform in the long run for those less scary paper losses in adverse conditions. If not, rejecting the ETF as a buy/hold vehicle and using it tactically makes more sense.

Which brings me to the outlook. Let's now see how current conditions shape the value proposition of this fund.

Outlook

Monetary Policy

In March, we got the update to the Summary of Economic Projections, which has the median Fed policymaker forecasting two rate cuts this year, down from three about five months ago. Since then, inflation has been decelerating and real GDP growth has remained solid, with a labor market in an overall decent condition.

So, why the forecast deceleration in rate cuts? Basically, the central bank is reluctant to return to easing as tariffs threaten to reignite upward pressure on the pace of price growth. And tariffs have been imposed too recently for the hard data to reflect their impact.

Soft data, on the other hand, indicate the potential issues that central bankers are wary of. Some of them have commented on how business contacts are ready to hike prices because of tariffs. Yes, growth is at risk too if demand growth decelerates; the consumer-sentiment index from the University of Michigan is at one of the lowest levels recorded going back to 1952.

However, the Fed cannot risk acting preemptively right now and risk undoing the progress on inflation. It did that last year and had to pause the rate cuts as inflation went up again; monetary policy stance volatility is unwelcome by the markets and can cause serious issues when it comes to general economic and market confidence.

So, for now, it's a wait-and-see phase for the Fed. Despite that, I now believe that the two rate cuts later this year can happen. This is not optimistic, but realistic. Tariffs are most likely going to impact inflation in the short term, but it is now at a low enough level for the increase to result in simply less modest inflation levels in the near term, not dangerously high ones. Remember, tariffs won't be accompanied by stimulus (the Fed keeps reducing its Treasury holdings) and the same labor market conditions observed back in 2020-2021.

In any case, while the Fed may start cutting after summer, it is unlikely to do so later this month. This might not be well received by the markets, although they mostly expect it. Having low-volatility holdings in one's portfolio seems reasonable for now.

Trade Policy

The most serious issue right now is what the final trade agreement between the U.S. and China will look like. It's highly unlikely that tariffs will return to triple-digit rates; that was just political posturing from both sides.

However, the current precarious state of the agreement, as both parties have continued to engage in a trade war even without resorting to tariffs, implies market volatility ahead. That's where ETFs such as LGLV can be useful.

Moreover, the trade friction between the European Union and the United States can add to the unpredictability relating to growth and inflation, as it is yet unclear where this is going exactly. President Trump's negotiating power has been weakened by a recent trade court ruling deeming most of his tariffs illegal. But he was still able to and did raise the tariffs on steel and aluminum to 50% recently, which has already made the E.U. state it is ready to react with countermeasures.

Sentiment & Positioning

The NAAIM exposure index, which shows how many active portfolio managers are bullish on U.S. equity stocks based on current allocations, quickly went up in the last month. However, the AAII allocation survey still shows that retail investors are relatively modestly positioned. This positioning divergence is reflective of a cautious approach, but is a bullish signal since professionals are positioned for upside.

However, there is a problem. Around the time managers started reallocating to equities, this is when it seems it was a good time to buy in retrospect, as the market climbed above its 50DMA a couple of weeks later. The current momentum appears to be high enough for professionals to maintain their exposure. But retail investors are skeptical and this is justified given current levels amid high economic uncertainty.

So, it's not surprising that in the past 6 months net fund flows for LGLV have been positive at $236.18 million and negative for SPY at $-14.57 billion. Investors have been and seem to remain defensive.

All in all, current conditions and sentiment indicate that a defensive position is justified. For this reason, LGLV is now likely to do better than the market as long as the uncertainty persists.

Your Turn

What do you think? Do you agree with this thesis or not? Let me know below.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: Avista: Maybe Boring Utility Returns, But Not With This Strategy
data.description:
Article Thesis

I initially covered Avista Corporation (NYSE:AVA) in October last year, when I assigned it a “Buy” rating, with the stock at ~$38. My Fair Value estimate was $43.07, based on a DDM (Discounted Dividend Model) and backed by comparisons with peers and with Avista’s five-year averages.

Avista is mostly a regulated utility, and utility stocks are usually boring, with some exceptions like 2024. That’s why I generally initiate a full position in a utility stock if the sum undervaluation + dividend yield + dividend growth is higher than 20%.

However, by using the power of options, I can have significant leverage. In this case, I sold a Put Spread, then transformed into a single Put. Like mentioned in my second article, this strategy brought about 44% return in about 5 months, compared to a single-digit return for the stock. With the stock around $40 in March and a Fair Value estimate of $42.56, Avista did not satisfy anymore my rule of 20% mentioned above for utilities.

Like mentioned in the second article, I was waiting for the stock to drop under $38 again. As posted through a note on May 14, I re-initiated a position at ~$37.5. This time I sold only a Put, but I do have the intention to transform into a Put Spread again. More about this strategy later.

Last Quarter Highlights

Avista is mostly a regulated utility (90% - 95% of their business) with operations in 4 states:

We can observe that in Washington they have new rates effective January 2025, for a ROE (Return On Equity) of 9.8%. For both Idaho and Oregon, they are expecting new rates to be settled in September this year, for proposed ROE of 10.4% and 9.5%.

The problem is that Avista has a more modest realized ROE of 7.27%, influenced by their unregulated part of the business, which is more exposed to short-term market price gyrations. Long-term, they do expect to see a return on these investments, and compared to 7.1% ROE in March, the trend is encouraging.

Q1’2025 EPS was $0.98, compared to $0.91 one year ago. For FY’25 they are reaffirming EPS guidance for $2.52 - $2.72, while consensus EPS is $2.56, a remarkable 11% YoY growth. However, this growth is partially derived from the new rates and from the losses incurred in 2024 in their unregulated segment.

According to Avista’s expected capital spend for 2025-2029, they continue to expect an annual rate base growth of 5% - 6%, while there are other U.S. regulated utilities expecting 7% - 9%, especially some Southern ones, which are favored by increased demand of electricity brought by the Data Center boom, but not only.

Avista maintains a BBB credit rating, stable outlook, from S&P Global. However, for comparison, stronger U.S. regulated utilities usually have a BBB+ credit rating.

On the other side of the capital structure, they issued $16M new equity in Q1’25 from a total of $80M expected for 2025. This will result in marginal ~0.5% dilution, which I consider normal for a utility.

Fair Value

For a relative valuation, let’s start with a comparison with Avista’s five-year averages:

For each industry, I look at the most relevant multiples, and for regulated utilities I think these are:

  • Price / Book (closer to Price / Rate Base): 1.17, compared to 1.28 5Y, denotes ~10% undervaluation.
  • EV / EBITDA (because it includes both profitability and debt, which are crucial for utilities): 10.06, compared to 11.72 5Y, implies about 16% undervaluation.

If we look at the same two multiples in comparison with the sector:

  • EV / EBITDA gives about the same undervaluation.
  • Price Book: 1.17, compared to 1.85 for the sector, would give us a more significant undervaluation. However, this is not very reliable in this form, it’s important to look at this multiple together with ROE and, like I mentioned before, Avista has a lower ROE than other regulated utilities.

For an absolute valuation, I use a DDM (Discounted Dividend Model) in two stages. For the first five years, I think that consensus dividend estimates are a little too optimistic:

That’s because they raised the dividend only by 3.2% in February, due to a high payout ratio (about 75% expected for 2025), and they will keep increasing the dividend at a slower pace than their EPS, trying to bring down their payout ratio to about 70%. That’s why I’ll model only 3.5% growth:

Fair Value

My Fair Value estimate is $42.12, about 10% potential upside from the current price ($38.29), while the relative valuation gives about (or at least) the same numbers: 10%-16% potential upside.

If we add a 5.2% dividend yield and 3.2% dividend growth, we get a total of 20.3%, only marginally above my 20% threshold. That’s why I assign it a “Buy” status under $38.

A Balanced Leveraged Strategy For a Boring Utility

Since I consider it as a “Buy” under $38, as posted in my note from May 14, with the stock at ~$37.5, I sold a 35 Put expiring December ’25, for $218.

However, there are some important differences between a “cash-type” account and a “margin-type” account:

Cash account: I need to set aside $3500 (since I could be assigned). Subtracting the collected premium, we have a cost of $3282, and a maximum ROI of 218 / 3282 = 6.6%, or about 12% annualized. A covered Call (100 shares and a short Call) gives slightly better results in this period, with the Call prices higher than Put prices due to high interest rates.

Margin account: I am required to cover only the margin; however I like to be more prudent, I want to set aside more cash. I usually look at what I think to be the worst-case scenario. I can’t see Avista under $25 (more about risks later) until December. At $25, they would have a 7.8% dividend yield, outstanding for a regulated utility, even if there will be no growth.

So, I set aside $1000 (3500 – 2500). If we subtract the collected premium of $218, we have a cost of $782 and a maximum ROI of ~28%, or about ~52% annualized. This is significantly more than the return in a cash account. Yes, for other stocks we could have even more, but also with a higher risk than for a regulated utility.

Some possible outcomes at expiration:

As usual, I will very likely close the position before expiration, with little extrinsic value left. For example, if the Put will value only $50 in September, it’s not worth it to keep it another three months, to avoid a price reversal.

Full position: However, this Put is not enough for a full position, I will likely complete it with some shares. There are several reasons for that:

  • Avista has very illiquid options; I got a good price on May 14, but I might not be able to get it again, even with the stock under $38, especially with some time passing.
  • As you can see in the table above, in the unlikely scenario with Avista going towards $30 (and especially below $30), the Put loss (in a margin account) is higher than the stock loss.
  • The stock also performs better than the Put at ~$50 or above (even if another unlikely scenario).

So, I’m watching closely these days if I can buy some shares under $38, but still not for a full position.

Another possibility is to combine these new shares with buying a 30 Put, so transforming the short 35 Put into a short 35/30 Put Spread. But again, it depends on the premium that I could get. If I have to pay $100 for the 30 Put, that means a collected premium of 218 – 100 = $118. With a cost of 500 (cash needed to set aside if both Puts will finish In-The-Money) – 118 = $382, maximum ROI would be 118 / 382 = ~30%. So, any price lower than $100 for the 30 Put would be interesting, with better returns from the Put Spread than from the short 35 Put.

And I will initiate a third round (shares or options) only if the stock will go down more, without major fundamental shifts (averaging down).

Risks

There is a bit of uncertainty about their pending rate cases in Idaho and Oregon, let’s suppose they won’t get a favorable rate. Coupled with the more capricious unregulated business segment, let’s say they will need to slow down even more their dividend growth. With 2.5% dividend growth for the next 5 years, Fair Value would be $40.3, still very slightly undervalued, but less than my 20% threshold for a new position. So, I won’t increase my position, and I won’t renew my Put. However, I would keep my existing position (considering I’ll acquire some shares in the meantime), for which I require only 10% combined undervaluation + dividend yield + dividend growth.

Can I be assigned for the short Put before expiration? Yes, that can happen occasionally, if the position is ITM (In-The-Money), of course, meaning the stock under $35, and I could get 100 shares, which will drag $3500, significantly more than the $1000 that I set aside. But that’s very simple, I will instantly close the 100 shares after the assignment notice and roll over another Put.

Related to the cash needed to set aside for the Put option, I already said that I tried to imagine the worst-case scenario until December, with Avista at $25. Now, can the things be even worse? Even highly unlikely, anything can happen in theory. For example, wildfires are a risk especially with the summer coming, but Avista has wildfire mitigation plans, together with the authorities from Washington or Idaho, so I don’t think that could be such a catastrophic event.

However, let’s suppose that the unimaginable will somehow happen and Avista will go below $25 until December. We need to look at this in the dynamics of a portfolio. I have several short Put positions and a total cash and equivalents position which is significantly greater than the sum of cash set aside for these Put positions. So, if one of these short Put will go below my worst-case scenario, it won’t affect too much my entire portfolio.

Takeaway

Avista is mostly a regulated utility business, with increased rates in 2025 in Washington and pending rate cases in Idaho and Oregon. A 5.1% dividend yield and modest 3% - 3.5% dividend growth support my investment thesis, despite lower ROE versus peers.

Derived from a DDM and backed by comparisons with Avista's 5-year averages and with the sector, my Fair Value estimate is $42.12, offering ~10% potential upside, besides the dividend. With a total undervaluation + dividend yield + dividend growth = 20.3%, I rate the stock a "Buy" under $38.

For more appealing returns, I sold a $35 Put Dec'25, which I expect to yield a maximum of ~28% ROI, or 52% annualized (in a margin account, considering the worst-possible case with Avista at $25 until December).

I am planning to transform this Put into a Put Spread by buying a $30 Put if I can get a favorable price, and also to add some shares, partly on another pullback under $38, partly only on a bigger drop.

data.title: Cactus Looks To Face Off Challenges
data.description:
WHD Is On A Tight Path

In my previous article on October 6, 2024, I discussed Cactus, Inc.'s (NYSE:WHD) strategies. After Q1, new products in wellhead systems design, supply chain alternatives, and ramping up production in select facilities. Through these initiatives, the company will minimize the effects of higher tariffs if implemented. Over the past years, the company improved drilling efficiency and production sold per rig. The potential acquisition of SPS can significantly enhance Cactus’s geographic presence and strengthen the pressure control business.

The US onshore drilling activity remains under pressure as global uncertainty persists. WHD’s cash flows weakened in Q1. Despite the challenges, WHD's strong balance sheet allowed for increased shareholder returns. I expect an operating margin to stabilize in the medium term, which validates my 'hold' call for the stock.

Why Do I Keep My Call Unchanged?

I considered WHD a "hold" in my article published in October 2024. I wrote:

As volume growth becomes challenging, WHD introduces a new wellhead system and frac valve design to reduce drilling times and maintenance costs. It seeks to improve its cost structure and risk profile. It will also benefit from the Cactus supply chain's positive impact in sourcing certain FlexSteel product components. As a result, its operating margin expanded in Q2.

Since then, offering new wellhead designs and ramping up production facility has become a critical component for improved efficiency. Drilling activity has remained constrained in the US, but international shipments have started to make a recovery. With a robust balance sheet and weaker cash flow, I retain my "hold" take on the stock.

Industry Drivers And WHD’s Responses

WHD has lost some momentum in recent months. Unsurprisingly, the decline in energy activity levels and the US rig count has taken a toll on Cactus’s business, which gathers strength from producing new wellhead systems and new frac valve designs. The US rig count dropped by 4.4% year-to-date, while the US frac spread count dipped by 5.5%. As a result, the company’s levels of product sold per rig also moderated. The company estimates that its customers will likely reduce their Q2 onshore drilling. Quantitatively, it expects to exit Q2 with ~560 rigs, compared to 590 in Q1. Moving forward, the fall will persist as customers adjust their capex, energy prices remain pressured, and the tariff impact takes full toll.

By 2026, WHD expects to neutralize the tariff effect on its cost structure. By that time, it may have explored alternative sources, rolling out new wellhead designs, and ramping up its Vietnam production facility. Since the current tariff situation is volatile, although a truce on the tariff war has reportedly been reached with China. Investors may note that products imported from Vietnam currently incur a 25% duty. While it will increase the operating cost in the short term, I expect the impact to be minimized once the dust settles and the company fully implements the countermeasures.

Cactus to Acquire BKR’s Pressure Control Business

In June, WHD announced that it agreed to acquire 65% of Baker Hughes’s Surface Pressure Control Business (or SPS), which designs and manufactures surface pressure control solutions. The acquisition value is $344.5 million. It will form a joint venture with BKR. BKR will initially retain 35% of the JV ownership, but WHD can purchase the remaining 35% after two years.

The acquisition can vastly improve Cactus’s geographic footprint in the pressure control business because it has a relatively minor presence outside of the US, while SPS generates approximately 85% of its sales in the Middle East. The acquisition will also add more than $600 million to Cactus’s backlog. SPS has a capital-light business model with highly engineered products sold directly to end-users.

Near-Term Outlook

Based on the industry dynamics discussed above, WHD expects Pressure Control revenues to decline by “low to mid-single-digits” in Q2 compared to Q1. The adjusted EBITDA margins in the segment are expected to remain stable at 33% to 35%. In the Spoolable Technology segment, the company typically experiences a sales growth in Q2, although, in 2025, activity decline can partially clip it.

Order rates improved in Q1 despite industry weakness, as the private and small E&Ps' purchasing behaviour tends to remain resilient during an energy price downturn. So, the management expects revenue to increase by “mid-to-high single-digits” in Q2, backed by improved order rate in Q1. In the Spoolable Technology segment, the adjusted EBITDA margins may contract to the range of 35%-37% in Q2, which would be a marginal drop from 36.2% in Q2.

My Estimates

Over the past 17 quarters, its adjusted EBITDA increased by 10% on average. Over the next few quarters, I expect the EBITDA growth to stabilize due to energy activity moderation, mitigated by the company’s strategic moves. However, its topline growth can stall. I also think the SPS acquisition can enhance WHD’s sales and reduce its cost structure. The acquisition can provide access to new non-tariff-impacted markets in Pressure Control and Spoolable Technologies products. While I expect the stand-alone WHD to increase adjusted EBITDA by 7%-9%, the acquisition, if executed, can push it higher.

Analyzing Q1 Drivers

From Q4 2024 to Q1 2025 (as released in its Q1 2025 earnings on April 30), WHD's revenues in the Pressure Control segment increased by 7.7% because of increased drilling efficiencies that resulted in products sold per rig. Adjusted EBITDA margin contracted by 80 basis points in Q1 after the company booked reserves in connection with litigation claims.

Revenues decreased by 3.6% in Spoolable Technologies due to a typical lower domestic customer activity. Adjusted EBITDA margin contracted by 60 basis points from Q4 to Q1 due to lower operating leverage. However, the segment benefited from increased international shipments in Q1.

Cash Flows and Balance Sheet

Cactus's cash flow from operations decreased by 52% in Q1 2025 compared to a year ago, led by lower revenues in the past year. Free cash flow also dropped by 62%. It is also increasing inventory in the Spoolable business in anticipation of seasonally stronger Q2 and Q3 activities.

The company reduced its FY2025 capex guidance to $40 million - $50 million. Much of the capex would be directed toward its manufacturing diversification strategy and enhancing efficiencies in the Baytown manufacturing plant. As of March 31, 2025, WHD's liquidity (cash balance plus available credit facility) was $570 million. It may increase its FY2025 capex if the funding proves to be strong.

Relative Valuation And Target Price

WHD's forward EV/EBITDA multiple versus the current multiple is expected to remain unchanged. Ditto with its peers. This usually results in a similar EV/EBITDA multiple compared to its peers. However, its EV/EBITDA multiple (8.1x) is higher than its peers' (CHX, THNPY, and NOV) average of 5.9x. So, the stock is relatively overvalued.

WHD's five-year average EV/EBITDA multiple is 13.9x. If the trades at that multiple, the stock price can rise by 80% from the current level, as I described in my previous article. If it trades at the peers' average, the stock price can decrease by 16%. Since my last publication in October 2024, where I suggested a "Hold," the stock has decreased by approximately 30%.

As I discussed earlier in the article, I expect 7%- 9% adjusted EBITDA growth in the next four quarters (without the acquisition impact). Feeding these values into the EV calculation and applying the current EV/EBITDA multiple of 8.1x, I think the stock should trade between $52 and $53, implying an 18% upside.

Wall Street Rating

Three sell-side analysts rated WHD a "buy" (including a "strong buy"), four analysts rated it a "hold," and one rated it a "sell." The consensus target price is $50.9, suggesting a 14% upside at the current price. Given the company's operational recovery, I think Wall Street analysts are realistic about their expectations.

What's The Take On WHD?

Early indicators suggest that Q2 energy activities will remain subdued as operators take a cautious approach following the decline in energy prices and global uncertainty. The tariff rise worsens the situation. To counter the challenges, WHD will fall back on strategic moves, including alternative supply chain sources, new wellhead designs, and increasing production capacity at a few facilities. As a result, its operating margin stabilized in Q1, and the stock outperformed the VanEck Vectors Oil Services ETF (OIH) in the past year. The SPS acquisition, if executed, can help minimize the tariff impact and improve its margin.

WHD has improved drilling efficiencies over the years, resulting in higher production sold per rig. Although cash flows slid in Q1, its liquidity base remained robust. It has also stayed its course on returning capital to shareholders. Given its rich relative valuation, I rate the stock a "hold."

data.title: Hewlett Packard Enterprise: AI Momentum Builds, Hybrid Cloud Strengthens, Valuation Still Low
data.description:
Investment action

I give a buy rating for Hewlett Packard Enterprise (NYSE:HPE). I believe the accelerating AI systems demand, strong hybrid cloud momentum, and recovery in intelligent edge point to more sustainable growth ahead. The Juniper acquisition is an important catalyst that could drive meaningful growth acceleration. Valuation is also at a meaningful discount to peers, and hence, I think the setup looks favorable from a risk-reward standpoint.

Company description

HPE is an IT infrastructure company that offers solutions for businesses to manage data across various environments. HPE sells a variety of products, including things like networking, storage, compute, and AI-enabled hybrid cloud solutions. Its customer base ranges from SMBs to large enterprises and government agencies.

2Q25 Earnings

HPE's total 2Q25 revenue came in at $7.6 billion, up 7% y/y in constant currency, and this was driven by y/y growth across all segments. Servers saw $4.1 billion in revenue (up 7% y/y in constant currency), while Intelligent Edge grew 8% y/y to $1.2 billion, and Hybrid Cloud was the fastest grower at 15% y/y to $1.5 billion. While revenue performance was good, margin performance was less exciting. Adj. gross margin fell 370 bps y/y to 29.4%, while adj. EBIT margin came in at 8%, down 150 bps y/y. The server segment margins fell sharply to 5.9% (from 11% a year ago), while the intelligent edge margin expanded to 23.6% (from 21.8%), and the hybrid cloud margin expanded nicely to 5.4% (from 1%). Although adj. EPS was down 10% y/y to $0.38, it still beat the high end of guidance ($0.28–$0.34).

AI server momentum, edge recovery, and hybrid cloud growth

In my opinion, the most exciting aspect of the HPE equity story is the strong and accelerating demand momentum in AI systems. In 2Q25, HPE recorded > $1 billion in AI systems revenue, up from $900 million in 1Q25, and net new AI systems orders saw $1.1 billion, driving total AI backlog to $3.2 billion. To give context on how substantial this $3.2 billion is, it is 78% of FY24 AI systems revenue, and the even more impressive data is that management noted the AI pipeline is in multiples of the current backlog. Readers can assume any multiple that they like, but say it is 2x; it means backlog + pipeline is more than the entire FY24 AI systems revenue, which gives significant visibility.

The mix behind the AI systems orders is also very positive. About one-third of the new AI orders came from enterprise customers, which I take as a sign that HPE has successfully diversified away from just selling to hyperscalers and is now able to sell into broader corporate use. This is very important on a few grounds: (1) Because enterprise deals often involve more services and customization, these mean more margin upside; (2) the negotiation landscape tilts in favor of HPE as it is no longer negotiating against a few large players; and (3) growth ahead will become less volatile as HPE is not as dependent on hyperscaler capex cycles.

Aside from AI, HPE’s Intelligent Edge and Hybrid Cloud segments also deserve a highlight. The Intelligent Edge segment has finally returned to y/y revenue growth, and given that this was also the third straight quarter of y/y order growth, I’d say it is very well on its recovery track and suggests a broader recovery trend in enterprise networking (which was in a downtrend in 2024).

As for hybrid cloud, this segment continues to deliver solid results, and I believe it can continue. The Alletra MP platform. To give a quick overview of what this platform is about, Alletra MP is essentially a data storage system, but it has more flexibility and is built to also address future use cases. The “MP” stands for multiprotocol, and this basically means customers can scale or swap out parts of the system without having to rip and replace the whole thing. In short, customers enjoy a lot of modularity, which is important for large enterprises trying to modernize storage without replacing their entire IT infrastructure. The platform is also able to support AI workloads, so it really addresses the needs of enterprises that want to upgrade their IT infrastructure to facilitate AI capabilities without a major overhaul. The numbers don’t lie. The Alletra MP platform has seen solid demand, with orders growing by more than 75% y/y for four straight quarters. This is a real sign that the platform is resonating with customers and gaining traction.

Juniper Acquisition is a Transformative Catalyst.

One cannot talk about HPE without discussing the Juniper acquisition. This is a very important catalyst in HPE’s equity story, and in my view, it could be a game-changer if it goes through. The current expectation is for the deal to close by the end of FY25, and management is targeting at least $450 million in annual run-rate cost synergies within three years post-close. But the focus is not the cost synergies, but the fact that this acquisition would instantly give HPE a stronger AI-networking portfolio that enables HPE to better compete for AI solution deals. Furthermore, the acquisition also provides HPE direct access to a sizable enterprise customer base—and this opens up a path for cross-selling HPE's existing solutions as well.

Valuation

Using consensus estimates is a fast and effective way to value HPE stock, since they have been fairly accurate over the past 10 years. Say consensus remains accurate; HPE should do $2.34 adj. EPS in FY28. This is about 8% earnings CAGR from FY25 guidance of ~$1.84. Add the dividend yield of ~3%, which equates to a pre-valuation multiple change total return of ~11%. Now, what multiple should HPE be worth? Peers in the industry with similar levels of EPS growth, like Cisco Systems (CSCO), International Business Machines (IBM), and NetApp (NTAP), trade at 13x and 23x forward PE, respectively, but HPE only trades at 9x. The lower profit margin (single-digit EBIT margin vs. peers at double-digits) and overhang from the Juniper acquisition uncertainty may be the reason for the discount, but say multiple stays at the current level, investors can still make ~11% total return/year, which, I think, is quite attractive.

Risk

The Juniper deal is not certain yet as it is being held up by regulatory scrutiny, and I see it as a real risk that the deal may not go through. If the deal were to fall apart or face prolonged delays, it would remove a major growth lever and could really hurt near-term growth expectations.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for HPE. AI systems momentum is strong, hybrid cloud growth should continue to be driven by Alletra MP, and intelligent edge is well on the recovery path. Add to that a ~3% dividend and low valuation (~9x PE), and I think the total returns per year are attractive. If the Juniper deal goes through, the upside could be even more compelling.

data.title: Brookfield Asset Management Uncovers Hidden Value From Infrastructure And AI
data.description:
Brookfield Asset Management (NYSE:BAM) is no ordinary asset manager. It is the capital-light heart of a much larger ecosystem led by Brookfield Corporation (BN), a conglomerate that manages assets in infrastructure, real estate, energy and financial services worldwide. BAM was spun off from Brookfield Corp in late 2022 but remains majority-owned by that parent company, which owns 73% of the shares.

Since the beginning of 2023 BAM has outperformed, delivering a total return of more than 91%, far ahead of the S&P 500’s 51% over the same period. This performance reflects the market's appreciation of Brookfield's stable fee income and growth opportunities.

For investors who value fundamental growth, predictable income and exposure to real assets, BAM offers a rare combination. That is also the view of Pershing Square (the hedge fund led by Bill Ackman) which took a large stake in Brookfield Corp in early 2024. In its shareholder letter, they explicitly called BAM “an exceptional capital-light growth company” that they believe will drive Brookfield’s future returns. BAM is said to account for about two-thirds of BN’s valuation, despite generating only a small portion of operating cash flow.

Inflation Proof Income From AI, Infrastructure And Private Credit

While Brookfield Corp may seem complex with stakes in listed subsidiaries such as BIP, BEP, and BBU, real estate funds, and a fast-growing insurance arm, BAM itself is exceptionally clear. It makes money by receiving management fees on more than $549 billion in fee-paying assets under management, mainly in infrastructure, renewable energy, real estate, and private credit. This capital comes from institutional investors, pension funds, and insurance companies. BAM itself invests very little; it earns fees and performance bonuses with no significant balance sheet risk. This capital-light structure ensures high margins, low debt, and predictable income streams. An ideal profile in a world where investors value stable cash flows with inflation protection.

Brookfield Asset Management is fully positioned on structural global megatrends: energy transition, digitalization, reindustrialization and deglobalization. BAM manages billions of infrastructure projects in energy, transport and data centers. In 2025, it entered into a €20 billion partnership with the French government to roll out AI infrastructure, following earlier alliances with Microsoft, Intel and Deutsche Telekom.

These partnerships are not isolated deals but a recurring pattern: governments and large corporates choose BAM because of its reputation as an ‘operator’, a party that not only brings money but also execution, speed and trust.

In addition, BAM is growing rapidly in private credit: providing loans outside the banking system. Due to higher interest rates and stricter banking regulations, companies are looking for alternative financing. BAM now has more than $320 billion under management in credit capabilities, making it one of the largest players in the world.

Compared to its peers, BAM has its own positioning. Blackstone is larger in fee-bearing capital (~$860 billion) but relies more heavily on retail funds such as BREIT which are sensitive to drawdowns and market pessimism. KKR combines asset management with balance sheet ownership of companies and insurers, which brings additional leverage and volatility.

Brookfield’s strength lies in its long-term capital, which is typically tied up for 10 years or more with institutional partners and insurance transfers as the dominant source. It also has an internal ecosystem: via Brookfield Corp, group capital co-invests in the funds, which increases alignment with clients and improves access to deals.

So BAM does not offer “fast growth” but consistent and compound growth. In a world of liquidity and volatility, that is what many investors are looking for.

Record Growth In FRE As $10 Billion In Performance Fees Looms On The Horizon

In the first quarter of 2025, Brookfield Asset Management posted its highest quarterly growth since the spin-off from BN. Fee-related earnings (FRE) increased 26% compared to Q1 2024 to $698 million, or $0.43 per share. Distributable earnings [DE] came in at $654 million or $0.40 per share, up 20% YoY. This gain was driven by higher fee income across infrastructure, real estate, credit and energy. The FRE margin is exceptionally high, partly because the company has to incur almost no operating costs to collect management fees on capital already raised.

Fee-bearing capital rose 20% YoY to $549 billion. In Q1 2025, BAM raised $25 billion in new capital, including $6 billion for its flagship real estate fund, which now manages $16 billion. This is remarkable given that many real estate managers are struggling to raise funds in the current interest rate environment. It underscores Brookfield’s status as a partner of choice among institutional investors.

Brookfield deployed $16 billion of capital in the quarter and monetized $10 billion of assets, generating $9 billion in equity proceeds. This rotation demonstrates that BAM continues to actively create and unlock value even in challenging markets.

With $119 billion in committed, uninvested capital (‘dry powder’), BAM has a line of sight to hundreds of millions in future fee income without having to win new customers. According to management, this is the engine of FRE growth towards 2026 and beyond.

In addition, 95% of the capital under management is long term or permanent in nature. As a result, the income is virtually immune to market volatility or temporary outflows.

An important element that is often underexposed is the distinction between fee-related earnings (FRE) and carried interest (CI). FRE is the stable core: fixed management fees on assets under management. BAM bases its dividend and predictable cash flow on this. Carried interest, on the other hand, is performance-dependent and only follows when the underlying funds successfully sell their investments above a predetermined threshold return.

According to Pershing Square, the current market valuation barely includes any value for carried interest. While BAM earns a third of all performance fees on funds raised since 2023, that revenue stream is not yet visible because major exits have yet to occur. The first “harvest years” of BAM’s new vintages are expected from 2026 onwards, and according to Brookfield itself, could generate more than $10 billion over 5 years. If that happens, it will lead to a sudden jump in profitability.

For investors who get in now, carried interest is therefore a free call option: if BAM delivers, a second, more cyclical profit engine will be added on top of the stable FRE. That makes the difference between a “good” asset manager and an excellent compounder. And even without factoring in carried interest, BAM offers compelling dividend income.

Dividend, Valuation and Outlook

BAM increased its quarterly dividend by 15% to $0.4375 in 2025, or $1.75 per share on an annual basis. That gives a dividend yield of around 3.1% at the current price. The payout ratio is around 90% of distributable earnings, which is high but justified given the predictability of the income and the absence of heavy investment obligations. Brookfield is targeting 15% annual dividend growth through 2029. That would double the dividend within 5 years. In a market where most alternative managers either pay a low dividend (like KKR) or offer a highly volatile dividend (like Blackstone), BAM offers a unique combination of stability and growth.

With expected earnings per share of around $1.70, BAM is trading at over 33x times forward earnings. That is considerably higher than the sector average. Blackstone and KKR are trading at 29x to 24x times forward earnings. BAM is therefore priced as a quality grower with the expectation that fee income and margins will continue to increase.

That premium seems largely justified given BAM’s stable fee base, high operating leverage and the long-term nature of the capital under management. Nevertheless, it is important to look critically at the margin of safety. If the growth of fee-bearing assets slows down in the coming years or if performance fees fail to materialise, the multiple could fall without the underlying business fundamentally changing. In other words: this is not a turnaround story with hidden valuation, but a compounder with a lot of valuation already priced in.

A fair valuation of BAM requires an assessment of how fast its fee-bearing capital and profitability will grow, and what multiple investors are willing to assign to it. Since BAM largely consists of predictable fee income (FRE) and performance-based carried interest (CI), we use both as inputs.

Under a conservative scenario in which fee-bearing capital grows to $750 billion in 2029 (instead of the targeted $1 trillion), and with stable margins, BAM could generate around $3.5 billion in annual FRE. At 18x multiple (comparable to KKR), that yields a valuation of $63 billion. Add modest carried interest ($1 billion annually, at 10x) and about $2B net cash, and you get a total equity value of $75 billion, or around $49 per share.

In the base case (as Brookfield itself predicts) fee-bearing capital grows to $1.1 trillion and FRE rises towards $5 billion. At a 20x multiple on FRE and 10x on $2 billion CI, you end up with a total equity value of around $123 billion, or a price of $81 per share. That implies an upside potential of 44% from current levels.

In a bullish scenario where BAM raises FRE faster via insurance mandates and AI infrastructure, FRE could go to $6 billion and CI to $3 billion. A valuation of $165 billion is then in sight, which translates into a share price of around $108.

Given the current share price around $56, the downside is limited, and the upside potential is attractive, especially for investors with a 3–5 year horizon.

Conclusion

Brookfield Asset Management strikes a rare balance between defensive quality and structural growth. It offers access to the core of global capital flows in AI, infrastructure, and the energy transition without having to own real estate or energy projects outright. With a stable dividend, high margins, a robust balance sheet and a growth path towards $1 trillion in fee-paying assets, BAM is well-positioned.

For investors looking for growth with a backing, predictability, and a stock that delivers returns in both rising and falling markets, BAM is an excellent choice. The valuation is on the high side but understandable given the quality and visibility of future earnings. The scenario analysis shows that the downside risk is relatively limited, while the upside potential is up to 40% on a favorable growth path.

You don’t buy Brookfield Asset Management because it looks cheap today, you buy it because it is built to stay relevant tomorrow. With stability, scale, and exposure to structural growth, BAM has all the traits of a long term core holding. Therefore, I give BAM a buy rating.

data.title: EZCorp: A Decent Buying Opportunity Amid Macroeconomic Uncertainty
data.description:
Introduction

EZCORP (NASDAQ:EZPW) is a Texas-based Pawn lending company operating 1284 stores in the US and Latin America. In simple terms, EZPW's business model revolves around providing micro-sized non-recourse loans for a short time period to borrowers who are in urgent need of cash. In return, the company charges Pawn services charges, which are comprised of a fee and an interest rate charged for the loan. Moreover, the company also keeps collateral to secure the loan, which is often valuables like jewelry. Some of the time, the borrower fails to pay the loan; in that case, EZPW simply sells the asset that has been collateralized. Moreover, in some cases, EZPW also does outright purchases of an asset from customers for the purpose of selling instead of lending.

Pawn business is countercyclical

It is important to note that usually desperate people who are in urgent need of cash come to a pawn shop for either a loan or the sale of the asset. Hence, when there is stress in the economy or a general cash crunch among the lower strata of the economy, pawn businesses tend to benefit from this scenario as it increases desperate borrowing from the pawn shops.

Over the last few years, inflation has been a key concern in the US economy, which is still persistent, with the headline consumer price index (CPI) printing above the Fed's target range of 2%. Due to this, lower- and middle-income households are facing challenges like higher grocery and gas prices, while wage growth hasn't caught up to the higher cost of living. Due to this, pawn businesses, including EZPW, have reported extraordinary growth over the last couple of years.

Moreover, the need for pre-owned goods also rises in an inflationary environment as households become more cost-conscious and try to find bargains in pawn shops. The increase in demand for these goods leads to higher inventory turnover for the pawn shops and reduces the risk of inventory being stuck with the company.

While inflation is still a problem, the US economy is not in bad shape, with low unemployment numbers and economists revising their recession probability downwards. However, risks are still there, with a lot of uncertainty entailing from the Trump administration-led tariff war with the majority of its trade partners. In a nutshell, the macroeconomic environment is neither good nor too bad, but major downside risk is present, creating a good environment and prospects for pawn businesses.

An indirect beneficiary of gold boom

Over the last year, gold and silver prices have risen quite dramatically, with the gold and silver prices increasing ~60% since the start of 2024. Moreover, the gold price is yet to top out, with Goldman Sachs forecasting a $3700 target by the end of 2025. While EZCORP isn't a gold mining or an investment firm specializing in investing in bullions, its inherent business model allows it to benefit indirectly from the rise in gold prices.

First of all, a rise in gold prices leads to a higher pawn loan value, which means a borrower could borrow a higher amount of loan with the same possession. A gold chain that was valued at $500 in 2024 would be valued at ~$800 now, which can obviously fetch a higher amount of loan against it. This leads to a higher average ticket size for the pawn businesses. Moreover, when the borrower fails to pay the loan back, the merchandise is forfeited to the pawn shop, and the increase in the price of merchandise during this period leads to inherent gain for the company. While the company also deals in non-jewelry merchandise for lending, the majority of its Pawn Loans outstanding are secured through jewelry assets.

Another quite important point to note is that EZPW holds a significant amount of gold in its inventory, which is held at its shop to be sold to customers. As per EZPW's investor presentation, 65% of its inventory held by EZPW is jewelry (gold or silver). This effectively makes EZPW an indirect long bet on gold prices.

Valuation and conclusion

As per Seeking Alpha's Quant valuation rating, EZPW is currently rated as a B+. While the valuation rating takes various valuation ratios into account while arriving at the grade, I focus more on the price-to-book ratio to value a business that is focused on lending viewed in conjunction with return on equity (ROE).

EZPW is currently trading at a price-to-book (TTM) of 0.86x, which on the surface looks quite cheap; however, the 5Y average of 0.65x shows that the company usually trades in this zone. Moreover, upon looking at the return on equity, EZPW's low P/B multiple seems to make sense. EZPW has an ROE of ~10.8% (TTM); that too is higher than the 5Y average of ~3.1%. However, I believe the recent ROE numbers are much closer to the sustainable ROE of EZPW given growth in PLO resulting in operating leverage. Moreover, efficiency gains and customer traction from the digital and technological advancements like the mobile application and e-commerce platform could act as a tailwind for the ROE. However, these initiatives could take a while to bear fruit; until then, I am assuming the current ROE as a sustainable ROE figure for the company.

Given a sustainable ROE of 10.8% and a P/B ratio of 0.86x, EZPW's yield (ROE/price to book) comes in at 12.55%, which is decent enough to issue a buy rating.

Risk

The Pawn industry faces a complex and evolving regulatory landscape, which can affect EZPW's operational metrics going forward. The pawn industry does not hold a good public image, so increasing regulatory pressures to protect borrowers is a normal assumption to make. In the past, EZPW has been fined ~$10 million for illegal debt collection tactics, including in-person visits to homes and workplaces. Although this fine is quite a history now, one could gauge the severity of the penalty from this. Given the strong regulatory environment against the pawn industry and EZPW's dodgy track record, I believe similar fines and penalties could be levied on the company going forward.

Conclusion

Given an uncertain macroeconomic environment and surging bullion prices combined with a reasonable valuation, I have a buy rating on the stock.

data.title: The Drivers Behind REIT Dividend Growth
data.description:
Last time, we looked at Realty Income (O) as a case study in how using earnings per share (EPS) to calculate payout ratios for real estate investment trusts (REITs) can produce misleading results.

As noted, some public data providers showed payout ratios for Realty Income well above 100%, which might alarm income-focused investors at first glance.

But as noted, the proper metric is adjusted funds from operations (AFFO), a non-GAAP measure that adjusts net income by adding back non-cash items like depreciation and amortization.

This is critical in real estate, where property assets lose book value on paper despite often appreciating in reality. It also excludes gains or losses from property sales, offering a clearer picture of recurring cash flow.

Realty Income is a flagship REIT, well known for its monthly dividend payments and its membership in the S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Index, having increased its dividend for more than 25 consecutive years.

Today’s piece builds on that by taking a closer look at the underlying mechanics that allow Realty Income and other high-quality REITs to grow their distributions over decades without compromising sustainability.

Dividends Must Follow Cash Flow

The chart below illustrates exactly what investors should hope to see from a high-quality REIT: consistent growth in AFFO.

While Realty Income’s dividend shows the familiar stair-step pattern of monthly increases over time, what’s most important is that this growth appears supported by a steadily rising AFFO base.

This relationship is key: dividends don't exist in a vacuum. They're funded by real operating cash flow, and here we can see that AFFO has grown roughly in tandem with distributions, especially since the sharp post-pandemic rebound beginning in 2021.

This alignment is what helps ensure long-term dividend sustainability. Without it, dividend growth would eventually come at the cost of balance sheet health.

Occupancy Rate Is Your Anchor

One of the most reliable indicators of a REIT’s health is its occupancy rate. This metric tells you what percentage of a REIT’s total properties are currently leased and producing rental income.

It’s calculated quarter to quarter as the number of leased properties divided by the total leasable properties in the portfolio.

What’s considered “good” varies depending on the REIT’s focus. For example, post COVID-19 an office REIT may naturally carry a bit more vacancy than one focused on essential retail. But in all cases, stability is key.

Realty Income continues to set the standard. As of March 31, 2025, its occupancy rate stood at 98.5%. That’s down just slightly from 98.7% at the end of 2024 and 98.6% a year prior.

In other words, almost no change, which is especially impressive when managing over 15,000 properties. That level of consistency offers a clear signal that tenant demand remains strong and that management is effectively executing renewals or filling vacancies quickly.

For income-focused investors, this stability is foundational. A high, steady occupancy rate anchors rental revenue, which in turn supports consistent FFO growth.

Over the long run, we believe investors should look for REITs with occupancy rates consistently above 95% and minimal quarter-to-quarter volatility.

Same Store Rental Revenue is Your Organic Growth Driver

While the occupancy rate provides a solid foundation, it doesn’t tell the full story. What really drives income growth is how much tenants are paying over time and whether those payments are increasing at a healthy pace. That’s where same store rental revenue comes in.

Same store rental revenue compares the income generated by a consistent set of properties over time, filtering out the noise from acquisitions or dispositions. It reflects the organic growth of the portfolio by assessing whether existing tenants are renewing at higher rents.

Ideally, those increases help outpace inflation over time. If inflation averages 2%, you want to see rental revenue growth comfortably at or above that level to preserve purchasing power.

Realty Income’s latest quarterly results show same store rental revenue rose 1.3% year-over-year across 14,702 properties. While slightly below the inflation benchmark, that still reflects a positive contribution to FFO and ultimately dividends.

In today’s tighter environment for tenants and rent growth, any gain, particularly across such a large base is notable. It suggests leases are being executed or renewed at favorable terms, reinforcing Realty Income’s reputation for operational discipline and tenant quality.

data.title: Diamond Hill Small Cap Strategy Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Commentary

Markets were mixed in Q1 as the new presidential administration introduced a flurry of activity, creating some uncertainty about the path forward. During the quarter, the broad Russell 3000 Index declined by -4.7%, marking the first quarterly loss since Q3 2023. The small-cap Russell 2000 Index experienced a larger decline of -9.5%, reflecting the tendency of smaller companies’ shares to show more sensitivity during periods of heightened macroeconomic uncertainty. From a style perspective, value stocks performed comparatively better across the cap spectrum. For instance, the Russell 2000 Value Index declined -7.7%, while the Russell 2000 Growth Index saw a larger decline of -11.1%. This near-term shift from growth to value appeared to reflect a broader investor preference for safety amidst significant uncertainty surrounding trade and fiscal policy developments in the US.

From a sector perspective, likewise, more defensive sectors in the Russell 2000 Index led in the quarter, while cyclical stocks fell out of favor. Utilities stocks were the index’s sole positive performer, up +5.3%. Conversely, information technology stocks were hit hardest, declining -18.4% — which, given the sector’s weight in the index, was a major contributor to the index’s negative Q1 return. Consumer discretionary stocks also fell -14.9%, while energy and communication services stocks fell -12.8% and -12.5%, respectively.

What began in Q4 — market leadership transitions in anticipation of potential winners and losers from a major shift in executive-level policy direction — vigorously continued in Q1. To some extent, economic data took a backseat in the quarter as investors seemed to focus narrowly on the likeliest implications of major trade policy shifts announced during the quarter by President Trump. Shortly after the quarter concluded, Trump announced a raft of tariffs to go into effect immediately on what he dubbed “Liberation Day.” As has been the case since Trump began increasing tariffs, countries are responding in various ways, with some reaching out to the US to negotiate better rates and others — notably, China — choosing to retaliate with their own high tariff rates. Many companies have preemptively announced significant investments in their US operations — ostensibly moves aimed at avoiding tariffs themselves. Ultimately, whether the “Liberation Day” moniker goes down in history or infamy remains to be seen. What is clear is markets are very nervous about the president’s apparent determination to wholesale (and possibly singlehandedly) rework the global trade landscape, even with countries that have historically been significant trade allies, like Taiwan. We’ll have more thoughts on potential direction from here later in this communication.

Meanwhile, the Federal Reserve held rates in March, citing the likelihood tariffs increase inflationary pressures, while cutting its growth expectations for US GDP in 2025. And the US dollar — which one would generally expect to be bolstered relative to other global currencies as tariffs go up (higher prices for US goods increase demand for US dollars with which to pay for them) — counterintuitively fell as tariff language toughened, perhaps signaling investors’ growing concerns tariffs will contribute to a stagflationary US economic environment with high inflation and low or sluggish growth.

While volatility is unsettling, it also introduces interesting investing opportunities for those who have been diligent in their research all along. We have no unique insight into how deep this correction will become, whether it will enter bear market territory or how long it will last. However, we know it has helped reset valuations and offered some compelling opportunities to purchase high-quality names at more attractive prices than we’ve seen in some time. Once the trade-related dust settles, time will tell which sectors and individual companies are winners and losers. In the meantime, we will continue deploying our time-tested approach, which is well-built for uncertain market environments like we’re currently seeing — and which we wouldn’t be surprised to see persist for the foreseeable future.

Performance Discussion

Our portfolio trailed the Russell 2000 Index in Q1. Relative weakness was concentrated among our industrials and financials holdings, which fell more than benchmark peers. Conversely, we benefited from our information technology exposure — both our below-benchmark exposure and our individual holdings, which held up better than the benchmark names.

On an individual holdings basis, among our top contributors in Q1 were Mr. Cooper Group (COOP) and UGI Corporation (UGI).

Mortgage servicing company Mr. Cooper Group executed well during a particularly challenging mortgage environment as interest rates have remained high relative to historical levels. Importantly, Mr. Cooper’s business model is specifically designed to help protect it from just such periods: As rates go up, the value of its mortgage-servicing rights business goes up, giving it the ability to continue generating healthy cash flows even as the mortgage industry overall may slow. Shares were additionally boosted by the announcement on the final day of the quarter that the company will be acquired by Rocket Companies in an all-stock deal projected to close in the fourth quarter of this year.

Natural gas and electric power utility UGI is benefiting from improved leadership under experienced, new CEO Bob Flexon, who has a solid strategy for improving UGI’s underperforming US propane business. We have confidence Flexon can maximize the value of each of UGI’s business segments and maintain our conviction in the company’s ability to shift its portfolio into more sustainable fuel types over the long term, allowing it to capitalize on its advantage in delivering alternative fuels to rural locations not easily served by gas pipelines.

Other top contributors included Taseko Mines (TGB), Insperity (NSP) and International General Insurance Holdings (IGIC). Canada-based copper mining company Taseko Mines’ Florence copper project is on track for production starting in Q4 2025, which should be transformational for the company. Shares of human resources and business solutions provider Insperity rose as investors were encouraged by the company’s improved 2025 worksite employee numbers. While we believe the company has work to do to prove the value of its partnership with Workday (WDAY), we are encouraged by its progress thus far. Global specialty re-insurance provider International General continues capitalizing on its strengths— an emphasis on long-term results, an underwriting-first culture and shareholder-friendly capital allocation — to drive solid fundamentals, which the market is beginning to appropriately realize.

Among our bottom individual Q1 contributors were Triumph Financial (TFIN) and Live Oak Bancshares (LOB). Triumph Financial provides banking and other services with a niche focus on the trucking industry — which has been hit particularly hard the last few years amid a long freight recession and now faces additional tariff-related challenges, should less trade result in less movement of goods. Shares were pressured particularly hard during the quarter. However, we maintain our conviction in the positive outlook for what we consider a resilient company that is well-positioned to grow long into the future.

Shares of regional bank Live Oak Bancshares were pressured as it faced credit issues during the quarter, as well as auditor questions regarding its review process. However, we believe the company has carved out an attractive lending niche to small businesses and could position itself well to become a go-to lender for small businesses in the US.

Other bottom Q1 contributors included First Advantage (FA), Civitas Resources (CIVI) and Allegiant Travel (ALGT). First Advantage, which provides employment background screening, identity and verification solutions, faces pressure related to the uncertain macroeconomic environment, which could weigh on demand for background screenings in 2025. Further, it is amid its integration of recent acquisition Sterling Check Corp., which has leveraged the balance sheet for the time being.

Shares of oil and gas exploration and production company Civitas were pressured as management announced it anticipates lower oil volumes in 2025. Regional airline Allegiant Travel fell against a challenging macroeconomic backdrop and concerns about consumer spending, which could hurt customer demand. Further, there are some concerns the recently announced US tariffs could impact Boeing’s (BA) ability to deliver new planes to Allegiant as expected. However, we maintain our conviction in Allegiant’s resiliency in the long term and are maintaining our position.

Portfolio Activity

As volatility picked up sharply in the quarter, we were active in the portfolio — and we anticipate that as volatility continues into Q2, we will likewise attempt to capitalize on compelling opportunities to reposition the portfolio for the period ahead. We initiated three new positions in Q1, including in Helios Technologies (HLIO), Titan America (TTAM) and Avanos Medical (AVNS).

Helios Technologies is a well-respected provider of specialized hydraulic and electronic components to construction and agricultural equipment manufacturers. The company is transforming itself into a more diversified provider of higher-technology integrated systems to its customers. Recent cyclical headwinds have pressured earnings, and some execution missteps, combined with significant capacity additions that the company hasn’t yet absorbed, have combined to weigh on the valuation. However, we believe as Helios’s end markets recover, the company should enjoy meaningful margin growth, which we believe is not reflected in the current valuation. We consequently capitalized on the opportunity to initiate a position in the quarter.

Titan America was an initial public offering during the quarter of Titan Cement International’s US business. We believe the business is an impressive, vertically integrated building materials company with a logistics network competitors would struggle to replicate. We further anticipate the company should benefit from current and planned infrastructure spending in its end markets for the foreseeable future and consequently chose to participate in the initial public offering in Q1.

Avanos Medical’s technology portfolio includes innovative pain management and respiratory and digestive health product offerings, which have good organic growth profiles — including post-operative pain-management solutions, minimally invasive interventional (or chronic) pain therapies, closed airway suction systems and enteral feeding tubes.

Recently, the company has made significant progress cutting costs and augmenting its product portfolio to improve growth and profitability. However, as it has struggled to perform consistently, shares have been pressured to a point at which we find the valuation compelling, particularly given its core products have strong positions in inherently attractive areas of the medical technology industry that we expect to grow. We believe Avanos is well-positioned under new leadership to utilize its clean balance sheet to deploy capital via accretive M&A and share repurchases.

We funded these purchases in part with the proceeds from our sales of transportation infrastructure company FTAI Aviation (FTAI), fresh egg producer Cal-Maine Foods (CALM), agriculture and transportation company Seaboard Corporation (SEB), HSA- focused bank Webster Financial (WBS) and runoff consolidator Enstar Group (ESGR) as their valuations were no longer compelling relative to the size of the potential opportunities ahead of them. We consequently redeployed our capital into better positioned alternatives.

Market Outlook

As we start Q2, uncertainty is as high as it has been, arguably since the pandemic’s earliest days. While markets struggle to digest the likeliest ramifications of the US’s newly announced tariffs — whether they are largely a negotiation tactic whose economic impact will moderate over time as countries and companies reach better terms with the US, or whether they are here to stay for the long term and we will indeed see a significant reshaping of the world trade order — we are watching several factors particularly closely. Foremost, we are monitoring the relationship between the US and China, which is front and center in this trade showdown. As we write, President Trump has threatened a 104% tariff on China in response to its retaliatory increase to 84%. Given the magnitude of the US and Chinese economies, seeing the two countries enter a full-blown trade war would have significant, widespread implications for many US companies, up and down the capitalization spectrum.

Further, the increased uncertainty across a range of macroeconomic policies — trade as well as domestic spending and the administration’s ongoing efforts to meaningfully shrink the size of the federal government — is prompting concerns about the consumer. Should tariffs prompt a significant spike in inflation and/or fiscal spending cuts go too far, consumers could rein in their spending.

Indeed, companies across various sectors have noted a weakening consumer, suggesting the economy is slowing. With the consumer powering a significant majority of the economy, further weakening may present a risk to the lofty double-digit corporate earnings growth the market had been expecting for 2025.

However, regardless of how uncomfortable such volatility can be, we are simultaneously encouraged by its impact on the valuations of some companies that we believe are particularly strong and well-positioned to weather the environment ahead — even if it requires further suffering before economic and market growth resume. Accordingly, we have sought to be active in this period, identifying high-quality companies with particularly solid balance sheets whose outlook over the next three to five years is positive in our view. As downward volatility has continued, we have further acted on our research efforts to introduce these names to the portfolio, with the aim of positioning the portfolio well for whatever the environment gives us in the period ahead — whether there is further downside to endure, or whether markets turn around precipitously. We believe, with hindsight, that this period may prove to be one that separates those investors with a rigorous, bottom-up process from those unwilling or unable to see through the near-term volatility to the possibilities beyond.

Our primary focus is always on achieving value-added results for our existing clients, and we believe we can achieve better-than-market returns over the next five years through active portfolio management.
