data.title: Tidewater: Riding Volatile Market Tides With Ease
data.description:
Nearly four months after my previous coverage, Tidewater, Inc. (NYSE:TDW) remains a solid investment despite market uncertainty. Its strong Balance Sheet and cash flow show its high capacity and sustainability. This can be supported by its well-diversified OSVs and geographical locations. In addition, the stock is now much cheaper, justifying its upside potential. Its technicals are still weak right now, but buying opportunities are visible.

TDW’s Decent Start

Tidewater, Inc. opened 2025 with good Q1 2025 results despite the challenges it faced, especially in the Americas. Its Q1 2025 operating revenue amounted to $333.4M, up by 3.8% YoY from $321.2M. Both revenue components increased, and vessel revenues remained the core driver also with a 3.8% YoY increase.

Its operations in the Americas experienced some setbacks, with a -14.2% YoY revenue decrease. Somehow this was still reasonable considering seasonal and operational factors. Weather conditions and dry-docking maintenance. However, lower drilling activities in the Gulf of Mexico and project start delays primarily affected its operations in the region. This led to a notable decrease in the available days and utilization rate.

On a lighter note, its operations in West Africa, the Middle East, and Europe remained strong, offsetting the weakness in the Americas and Asia Pacific. Aside from that, it was easy for TDW to adjust and adapt to market volatility, considering its fleet availability versus market demand and supply. Its huge global presence allows it to set strategic pricing. As we can see, TDW enjoys and maintains favorable day rates across all regions. Despite the decreasing oil prices like in Brent and Crude WTI, I believe the necessity of these energy sources will keep increasing in the coming years. Eventually, their prices may recover, which can positively impact oil exploration and OSVs.

Meanwhile, its vessel operating costs decreased, which can be attributed primarily to lower oil prices and fuel expenses. Its operating expenses increased, although at a manageable rate. The biggest change was in the income it generated from asset disposal. It decreased from $11.0M to $2.5M. So, the operating income. Yet, this asset disposal is not part of its core operations. If we exclude it, the actual operating income of the company should be $73M versus $71M YoY. Hence, the operating margin will stay relatively flat at 22% YoY.

Inflation And Tariff Concerns

The stubborn inflation remains a primary concern even for OSV operators. Inflation is still a positive value today, so the price is still generally increasing. If we account for the inflationary nature of new tariffs, inflation may regain its momentum. This can weaken purchasing power and production costs across industries. As a result, the aggregate demand may soften further, which may lead to negative spillovers even in the oil market. Why? Lower consumption can lead to lower production and shipping, which can also lower the need for oil and fuel. So, policymakers may have to rethink their drastic implementation of new tariffs.

On a lighter note, the Fed remains on guard. Its decision to keep the policy rate cut pause is a prudent move to stabilize inflation and cushion the impact of new tariffs in the long run.

Decreasing Oil Prices And OPEC Hike In July As Double-Edged Swords

WTI and Brent have decreased substantially in the past year. From my previous coverage to date, their prices have already dropped by -12%. I think they will stay below $70 considering the planned OPEC supply hike this July. It is already common knowledge that this oil cartel has a huge influence on global oil prices. And when oil prices are lower, there is lower motivation for oil exploration and production. So, the need for OSVs may also decrease.

On the flip side, I believe this has some positive spillovers. For instance, lower oil prices also lower fuel expenses across industries, especially shipping and logistics. This will lower the cost of transporting raw materials and finished products. At some point, it can counter the impact of new tariffs and help manage inflation. Lower oil prices and fuel expenses can help related industries set strategic pricing to stabilize their profitability and stimulate demand.

The thing is oil and natural gas are crucial in the evolving market landscape. Their demand will eventually recover and rebound, and so will their prices. Amid the digital revolution and tourism boom, increased data centers, electricity consumption, and mechanical generators can support them. But what really drives their price and demand is the fact that all economic aspects revolve around oil. From their economic and geopolitical role to global infrastructure relying heavily on oil-based fuels, they don’t have exact substitutes, not even renewables. After all, the oil industry is now investing in cleaner technologies like emission reduction, biofuels, and green hydrogen to remain profitable even under political and climate regulations.

Drivers

One of the core strengths of Tidewater is its strong Balance Sheet and cash flow despite its capital intensity. Its prudent expansion and vessel management allow it to stay profitable and liquid. As you can see, its cash has increased substantially in the past 11 quarters. It can cover all current liabilities, proving its high liquidity. This should not be surprising considering its strong FCF. Last Q1 2025, it amounted to $76M, which means that it converted 22% of its operating revenue into free cash. So, TDW has enough cash to cover debt repayments and common stock repurchases to increase its value as a stock.

Moreover, debt levels have decreased since Q4 2023. This shows TDW’s high capacity to cover scheduled repayments. What’s more interesting is that its Net Debt/EBITDA is only 0.65x, so TDW only needs a year or less to repay its debt at once. It is also worth noting that nearly 40% of the total amount will not mature within three years. So, TDW has a lot of time and capital buffer to prepare for repayments.

I also like the fact that its average fleet age of 12.6 years is still about three years younger than the market average of 15 years. At the 15-30 age range, the fleet is considered mature. But it will still be operational. For over 30 years, fleets can still operate, but there will be a very low utilization rate of about 20%. TDW has more efficient OSVs since younger ones may require fewer repairs and maintenance.

The challenge lies in the availability of younger fleets and the time needed to create new ones. At this point, TDW will still have about three years before its fleets become mature. But again, this is not something that should alarm investors, as I already discussed in my previous coverage. Considering the long useful life, TDW will still be fine. The fact that younger fleet supply remains limited gives a huge advantage to TDW. Its huge market presence allows it to dominate the global industry. That is why it is easy for it to adjust to market changes and set strategic pricing to stabilize revenues and margins.

Valuation

TDW has already decreased by -20% since my previous coverage. While market correction and oil price weakness played a role here, the decrease was a bit too stretched. On a positive note, it highlights the cheapness of this stock. Now, it is trading at a much more favorable price, making it an ideal entry point.

We can assess it relative to some important metrics like sales per share and cash per share. At this point, the stock trades at 1.64x its SPS, versus the historical average of 1.92x. If we use this to find its fair value, the TP will be $49.78, a 16% upside potential. A similar observation applies to the cash per share.

Technicals

TDW’s momentum is still weak but better than during its correction. The price is now above the 50-day SMA, while the 50-day SMA has already flattened a bit, showing a slight shift in sentiments. Yet, more confirmations may be needed as they are still below the 200-day SMA. Exceeding it will start bullish signals.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI mirror the price trend. At this point, we cannot detect any bullish or bearish divergence. After all, the price appears to be consolidating. This is quite interesting since it has to exceed $44-45 for the uptrend to continue. This may support its increase to the 0.236 Fibonacci Retracement at $49.29, near my P/S TP.

At this price, TDW shows buying opportunities. Its momentum still has enough allowance for a buy position, since the stock is not yet overbought. Hence, entering is logical.

Conclusion

Tidewater, Inc. is still a solid company with its huge global market presence and decent topline. Its strong Balance Sheet and relatively younger fleet age allow it to keep its dominance. In addition, it is cheap with a reasonable upside. Technicals are still weak but show more buying opportunities. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Ennis: Stable Dividend Payer In A Declining Industry
data.description:
Ennis, Inc. (NYSE:EBF) operates in the traditional printing industry, which is gradually shrinking as more businesses move to digital solutions. The company recently released its FY 2025 Earnings report. I last wrote about it in November 2023. Since then, the stock has given a small total return of 4.37%. While it’s not a growth company, Ennis attracted me with its strong 5.41% dividend, steady income, and debt-free balance sheet. Its stock price is also reasonably valued when compared to the overall market. The company has managed to stay stable by buying smaller companies and keeping costs low, but there’s not much room for big growth in the future. Since other similar companies are priced about the same, there’s no strong reason to expect Ennis’s stock to go up a lot. That’s why, at its current price, I believe it’s best to hold the stock rather than buy it now.

FY 2025 Company update

Ennis is continuing its strategy of acquiring businesses to keep its income relatively stable and reward its investors with a steady dividend program. Ennis recently released its Q4 2025 earnings and full-year results. The company's results YoY are lower than FY2024. Q4 2025 saw revenue of $92.7 million, which was about 5% lower than the same quarter last year. If we look at earnings per share (EPS), there was also a reduction from $0.39 to $0.35 YoY. For the full year, total sales reached $394.6 million, a decline of just over 6%, while annual EPS dropped slightly from $1.64 to $1.54.

Despite the drop in sales, Ennis managed to slightly improve its profit margins. Gross margin, what the company keeps after covering production costs, rose to 29.5% from 28.4%, thanks to steady pricing and lowering its costs.

However, overall profit and cash flow were still slightly lower, mainly due to fewer customer orders as some businesses continue to move toward digital alternatives. Even so, Ennis highlighted its strong cash position and said it remains focused on controlling costs.

Ennis continues to grow by buying smaller printing companies. In April 2025, it completed a $35 million purchase of Northeastern Envelope. Other recent deals, which included Printing Technologies and Wisco, contributed about $2.5 million in new sales during Q4, and $13.2 million for the full year. One recent acquisition is performing below expectations, but overall, new additions are helping improve profit margins. The company has already merged these businesses into its system, and it is looking into more acquisition opportunities.

Cash flow, dividend, and balance sheet

Ennis finished FY2025 with essentially zero debt and a net cash position, unlike many peers. This allows the company to have flexible use of its capital to fund M&A, pay dividends, or even pursue buybacks. Ennis currently pays a $0.25 quarterly dividend at a forward yield of 5.41%. The high yield is supported by strong cash flows. We can see that levered free cash flow is upward trending YoY, ending at $56.2 million in FY2025 from $53.2 million in FY2024.

We can see the positive free cash flow that the payout is well covered, even after special dividends, such as the $65 million dividend in 2024. Management is also putting a lot of emphasis on maintaining the dividend. In the graph below, we can see that historically the company has kept a stable dividend program.

Another positive for the company is Ennis’s conservative balance sheet. The company has $72.48 million in cash and minimal capital expenditure as the printing plants mostly depreciated. This financial strength gives the company flexibility to make more acquisitions without needing to borrow money. In 2025, Ennis returned $92 million to shareholders, including a generous one-time special dividend of $2.50 per share, as seen in the graph above, and it continues to pay a regular quarterly dividend of $0.25 per share. In short, shareholders get a secure income stream. The risk is slower growth as it is playing in a shrinking print market. This means that really you are choosing the stock for its cash yields rather than anything else.

Valuation

Ennis is not the type of stock you hold if you are looking for the stock value to increase. Over the last five years the stock has increased by 0.76% and over the last decade, it has increased by a small 5.97%. The printing industry is not a growth industry, but Ennis is successfully taking on the industry through acquisitions, maintaining a strong cost strategy and good profit margins, with which they are rewarding investors with safe and generous dividends. If we compare Ennis to peers in the industry, namely Deluxe (DLX) and Quad/Graphics (QUAD), we can see that it trades higher on sales and earnings ratios. Its price-to-earnings ratio (P/E) is 12.08, while Deluxe trades at just 4.3 and Quad/Graphics at 6.13. Ennis also has a price-to-sales ratio of 1.22, much higher than Deluxe at0.30,and Quad/ Graphics at 0.10. This shows that the market values Ennis higher for its financial strength and stability. Ennis has no debt, solid cash flow, and consistently pays dividends, which supports this premium. But because it trades at higher multiples and its growth is limited, the upside may be limited. Ennis is not expensive compared to the broader market but looks pricey next to similar companies in its industry. So if you are looking for growth, there may be better opportunities elsewhere.

Risks

Investors should be cautious that Ennis operates in a declining part of the printing industry. As more companies move to digital billing, email marketing, and online catalogues, demand for printed business forms, envelopes, and mailers continues to decrease. One industry study estimates global print volumes dropped by over 5 trillion pages between 2018 and 2023. While Ennis has stayed profitable by keeping costs low and buying smaller print companies, its core market is shrinking. These acquisitions help stabilise revenue, but they’re unlikely to drive meaningful long-term growth. With few growth drivers and a tough industry backdrop, valuation upside may be limited, even though Ennis stands out for its strong balance sheet and reliable dividends.

Final thoughts

Ennis offers investors a reliable dividend, strong cash generation, and a debt-free balance sheet. For investors seeking to increase their income gradually, Ennis remains an attractive stock. The downside is that there is minimal growth potential, and it is playing in a mature industry in secular decline. At current levels, I don’t see a catalyst that would significantly drive the stock higher, but the company’s fundamentals remain sound. For long-term investors looking for income and stability, Ennis continues to be a reasonable hold.
